behaviors.description: Rows with missing required fields are rejected and appear in the dead-letter queue with the original row number.
behaviors.justification: Unit -> Integration proof: failure is at a handoff between CSV parsing/validation and DLQ publishing of metadata (original row number), which a single-function unit test can miss. Integration-brake: no real OS/network/subprocess is required (can use in-process CSV input and DLQ sink), no AI/ML output, no multi-agent chain. Rejection gate: System is disqualified because behavior text only requires handling validation + DLQ routing with row metadata, not real external queue/network behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Run pipeline modules together with a small CSV fixture and an in-memory/fake DLQ sink. Assert rows missing required fields are excluded from normalized output and emitted to DLQ with exact source row numbers.

behaviors.description: The transformation correctly converts date strings in multiple formats (ISO 8601, US, European) to a single canonical format.
behaviors.justification: This is pure transformation logic inside one function/object boundary. No module handoff is required to expose the failure; malformed parsing/normalization bugs are local. Rejection gate: Integration is disqualified because behavior text is strictly about format conversion correctness, not cross-module state transfer.
behaviors.test_description: Table-driven unit tests for date parser/normalizer covering ISO 8601, US, and European inputs (plus ambiguity edges) and asserting canonical output or explicit validation error.

behaviors.description: A CSV file with 500,000 rows completes processing within the configured timeout without running out of memory.
behaviors.justification: Unit -> Integration proof: throughput/memory behavior emerges from parser + validator + transformer + writer interaction, not one function. Integration -> System proof: timeout and OOM are OS/runtime resource behaviors (wall-clock scheduling, process memory limits/GC pressure) that in-process mocks can hide. Rejection gate: Agentic is disqualified because correctness does not depend on AI/ML output quality.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the real pipeline process against a generated 500k-row CSV and real runtime settings; assert completion before configured timeout and memory stays below limit (no OOM/crash). Capture peak RSS and elapsed time.

behaviors.description: When the S3 bucket returns a transient 503 error, the pipeline retries with exponential backoff rather than failing the entire batch.
behaviors.justification: Unit -> Integration proof: failure appears at the interface between batch orchestrator and S3 client retry policy (error propagation + retry scheduling), not only in isolated math for backoff. Integration-brake: real network/S3 is not strictly required; a stubbed S3 client returning 503 then success can reproduce the failure boundary. No AI dependency, no multi-agent chain. Rejection gate: System is disqualified because behavior text requires retry semantics on 503, which can be validated with deterministic in-process stubs and fake clock.
behaviors.test_description: Wire orchestrator, retry middleware, and S3 client adapter with a stub that returns 503 for N attempts then success. Verify exponential delays, bounded retries, and batch continues after successful retry.

behaviors.description: Rows successfully written to PostgreSQL are not duplicated if the pipeline is restarted mid-batch.
behaviors.justification: Unit -> Integration proof: dedup depends on interaction among writer, checkpoint/idempotency logic, and DB persistence. Integration -> System proof: behavior explicitly requires restart mid-batch, which is subprocess/process-lifecycle behavior; real restart and durable state boundaries are needed to catch failures hidden by in-memory simulation. Rejection gate: Agentic is disqualified because no AI/ML output determines correctness.
behaviors.test_description: Run pipeline against a real PostgreSQL instance, force an actual process stop mid-batch, restart pipeline, then assert final table has exactly-once rows (no duplicates) and expected row count.
